data.title: Hess Corporation: Another Platform Begins Operations Soon (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Hess Corporation (NYSE:HES) announced that the fourth platform of the Guyana project is expected to begin operations later in the fiscal year. That duplicates the nearly same announcement from Exxon Mobil (XOM) the operator. This may well affect the takeover of Hess by Chevron (CVX), because the value of Hess increases as the cash flow increases. The arbitration could very easily value Hess using current values, whereas the merger agreement is already a year old. The valuation could easily begin with the larger cash flow of that fourth platform included which could make the value of Hess higher than what was agreed upon by Chevron and Hess originally.

A hearing on the arbitration that Exxon Mobil claims was triggered by the Chevron acquisition offer for Hess is now set for late May, with a decision expected a few months later. That should finally bring the current period of uncertainty to an end.

This likely overshadows the Hess earnings announcement.

Last Article

The last article noted that the advantage may lay with Exxon Mobil because the earnings of Hess grow materially with the addition of each platform. Depending upon how the hearing proceeds and the value of Hess is calculated, the cash flow usually begins at the present, which is now a year later. Lower cash flows from the past drop off, while the larger cash flows due to the addition of the fourth platform are now very close to the start. The addition of the next two platforms (about a year apart) are also closer as well.

It is very easy to see how the arbitration could come up with a higher value for Hess than what Chevron offered originally.

Also to be decided is whether the Exxon Mobil contract clause is activated to exercise this right as Exxon Mobil claims.

With this article, there is now a definite arbitration date with a likely goal as to when the decision will be made. That brings a fairly long time of uncertainty to an end.

Now, as to whether the length of time passed means that a second shareholder vote will be required is currently up for discussion. One would certainly think that, to be fair with shareholders, that should be the case. However, that course of action is far from certain. I have seen nothing that "guarantees" shareholders will get another say in this merger.

Hess Earnings

Hess earnings comparisons are currently dominated by the Guyana Project's growth rate. Therefore, the addition of the fourth platforms assures a strong of positive earnings comparisons in the near future until the next platform begins production next year. So, earnings growth is assured for some time to come.

Earnings are down from the previous fiscal year because oil prices have been steadily declining since a peak price in fiscal year 2022. The way things are going, the prices are likely to bottom within the next year or so. This project, as noted in past articles has some record low breakeven prices. So, it is likely to remain profitable when much of the industry is reporting losses.

This is a big deal for Hess, because the operator is Exxon Mobil. So, all Hess has to do is help with decision making and cash the checks as they come in.

United States Profitability

The part of the company operating in the United States has not been all that profitable. The part operated by Exxon Mobil is very profitable.

The above breakdown makes it clear that much, if not all, of the company's value is attributable to the Guyana operations operated by Exxon Mobil. This is even more remarkable considering that the North Dakota operations and the Guyana operations have the same (roughly) production levels. Together they account for roughly most of the company production.

As the Guyana project continues to grow, the need for the North Dakota operations becomes less unless there is a way found to make those operations more profitable.

Cash Flows

The joint venture cash flows will be a particularly important part of the valuation consideration. Generally offshore wells are long lived with relatively low decline rates compared with the unconventional business.

Cash flow before changes in working capital basically indicates the earnings power of operations. As was likely indicated by the net income breakdown, most of the cash flow has come from the Guyana partnership. But that also means that most of the merger value would be attributed to the Guyana Partnership. That is the cash flow that is growing the fastest over time.

Right now, the addition of platforms annually for the near future favors Exxon Mobil in the arbitration process. However, the weakening oil prices favors the Chevron position. It therefore will be interesting to see what decision the arbitration panel comes to and why.

Summary

The merger of Hess with Chevron has a fair number of uncertainties. Right now, the price of the Hess stock is tied to the actions of the Chevron stock price until this merger completes. The arbitration process could free up Hess stock to trade on its own. Long term, that could possibly be a winner for shareholders.

However, for the time being, this is a cheaper way to buy Chevron stock. While I personally prefer Exxon Mobil, the current pullback of stocks has made Chevron a buy idea as well. Therefore, this stock is a buy consideration as either a discounted way to purchase Chevron stock or as a way to trade on its own prospects. Right now, it would appear that potential shareholders win no matter what happens.

I personally do not like merger proposals that drag out this long. However, it would appear that the merger will resolve itself in the current fiscal year. Now whether it was worth the wait is up to the shareholder to decide.

Risks

With the recent stock market pullback, the risk of the merger coming undone does not appear to be a liability for shareholders as it might have been in the past. Hess has excellent prospects as an independent company. Probably the biggest risk is one or all of the parties head to court after the arbitration proceedings to drag this out longer whether or not they are allowed to based upon the arbitration agreement.

Remember, anyone can file a court action for any reason. Whether it is deemed to have substance and proceeds is another matter entirely. Court actions can be part of a negotiating process no matter what the filing looks like.

Any upstream operation is subject to the volatility and low visibility of future commodity prices. A severe and sustained downturn of those prices could materially change the outlook of the company.

This offshore project is unusually profitable. Therefore, development of Guyana will likely proceed no matter the future price outlook of oil.

data.title: Bicara: Stock Decline On FICERA Abstract Release Creates Buying Opportunity
data.description:
Bicara Therapeutics Inc. (NASDAQ:BCAX) released updated interim data from its phase 1/1b study using its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta in combination with KEYTRUDA [pembrolizumab] for the treatment of patients with 1st-line recurrent/metastatic [R/M] head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC]. Such data was released as a publication abstract on the 2025 American Society of Clinical Oncology [ASCO] Annual Meeting website. The stock price has taken a hit, and maybe it is a sell on the news event, or investors may have been expecting a more solid data release.

Regardless, this drop in stock price creates a buying opportunity. Why would that be the case? That's because I believe that this was an overblown reaction because the full data set from this phase 1/1b study has not yet been released.

This brings me to the next point, which is that the company is still on track to provide the full data set from this early-stage trial on Sunday, June 1st, 2025, at 3:00 p.m. CT/4:00 p.m. ET. This is expected to happen after it gives an oral presentation of this data at this event. The reason why I believe that this data is far more important than what has been released already is because it is going to involve an even more important efficacy endpoint compared to what has been released thus far. The company is going to release overall survival [OS] results in the full data set. Along with the release of duration of response [DOR] data. as well.

Another reason why the drop doesn't make sense is because the company has established a focus on targeting 1st-line recurrent/metastatic HNSCC patients with negative HPV-disease. Not only is this an unmet medical need, but the data released far exceeds that of historical benchmarks. Plus, the company is already enrolling this very same program in the phase 2/3 FORTIFI-HN01 trial for the very same patient population. There is an alignment that interim Objective Response Rate [ORR] can be used for Accelerated Approval [AA] and then OS to be used for full approval. The downside is that full approval is going to take longer to achieve because the primary completion date of this specific study is not expected until 2029.

Interim Data Of FICERA Doesn't Tell The Entire Story

Again, it is hard to surmise the drop in stock price of Bicara Therapeutics with respect to the release of updated interim data as an abstract from its phase 1/1b study using its drug ficerafusp alfa [FICERA] to treat 1st-line recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma patients. However, I believe that this was an overblown reaction, especially since the company is still expected to release the full data set from this early-stage trial after its oral presentation at the ASCO Annual Meeting.

Before going over the interim data released from this trial, plus any catalysts still expected as part of this entire program, I believe that it is first important to go over what this disorder is and what the possible market opportunity could end up being. Head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC] is a type of disorder characterized by the formation of malignant cells in the squamous cells of the head and neck area. This particular area can include the throat, neck, and mouth. It is important to note that it forms in the outer layer of the skin [mucous membranes], which lines the airways and intestines of a person's body. Depending upon where the cancer actually is, there can be the formation of abnormal patches and ulcers.

The global head and neck cancer market is expected to reach $8.1 billion by 2031. Another item to note is that cancer can metastasize or spread to other parts of a patient's body, such as the lungs or lymph nodes. If this happens, then the survival prognosis gets even worse and thus there needs to be new effective therapies to help these patients. Bicara believes that it may end up offering hope to them.

In order to see whether or not it is going to be able to effectively use FICERA + KEYTRUDA for the treatment of these patients with 1st-line recurrent/metastatic head and neck squamous cell carcinoma [HNSCC], it's running the ongoing phase 1/1b study. This particular study was intended to use this bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta antibody to treat EGFR-driven solid tumors. However, this biotech took a keen interest in specifically going after these patients with HNSCC. With that being said, the whole reason for the drop in stock price of the company was because it presented a publication of an abstract entailing updated interim results from this trial using the FICERA combination to treat these patients on the 2025 ASCO Annual Meeting website.

Honestly, the data released wasn't bad at all. Especially, in relation to the specific targeting of these 1st-line R/M HNSCC patients with HPV-negative disease. It was revealed with the negative-HPV patient population of 28 patients, with a cutoff date of December 16th of 2024, that there was a 64% Objective Response Rate [ORR] achieved. In essence, 18 out of 28 patients given this FICERA combination responded to therapy. It is my belief that data was either misinterpreted by the market itself or was a sell-on the news event. The reason why I make this claim is that the ORR noted above is an improvement above that of historical benchmarks for these HPV-negative HNSCC patients to date.

While it is not yet clear if this ORR data translates to prolonged OS, it is not going to be long for investors to see whether or not such an outcome can be obtained. This brings me to the next point, which is that Bicara Therapeutics is actually expected to release the full data set from this phase 1/1b study targeting these 1st-line R/M HNSCC HPV-negative disease on Sunday, June 1st of 2025 at 3:00 p.m. CT/4:00 p.m. ET after its oral presentation at ASCO.

Why the targeting of HPV-negative patients? That's because, in the 1st-line R/M setting, it accounts for 80% of the population. These are patients who are more than likely either smoke or chew tobacco. Plus, negative HPV for these HNSCC patients carries a worse prognosis than that of HPV-positive patients. Treatment options are not ideal and limited in that they might include: KEYTRUDA from Merck (MRK), ERBITUX [cetuximab] from Eli Lilly (LLY), and other chemotherapeutic agents. Another reason to remain bullish here is that the company is also already in the process of exploring the use of FICERA with KEYTRUDA in the ongoing phase 2/3 FORTIFI-HN01 study. This particular registration-enabling study is ideal because alignment was already achieved with respect to the FDA. In essence, it was agreed upon that an interim analysis of ORR could serve as a means to file for Accelerated Approval of FICERA for the treatment of these patients. From there, full approval could be achieved based on OS data. A good thing about this is that there might be a way for this company to obtain marketing approval for this bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta antibody a lot earlier than anticipated.

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Bicara Therapeutics had cash and cash equivalents of $462.1 million as of March 31st of 2025. The company is not likely to raise cash anytime soon because it has extended cash for a long time. It believes that its cash on hand will be enough for a cash runway to fund its operations into the 1st half of 2029. Of course, that is barring it doesn't need to raise cash earlier than expected to fund another corporate strategy that has yet to be revealed.

Since its inception as a company in 2018, it has raised cash through the sale of common stock with its IPO, plus other financing events such as redeemable convertible preferred stock in private placements and debt offerings. The company burns roughly $41.8 million in cash per quarter. This is broken down into $34.3 million in R&D expenses and then $7.5 million for G&A expenses.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Bicara Therapeutics. The first risk to consider would be with respect to the upcoming full data set from the phase 1/1b study using FICERA in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat patients with 1st-line R/M HNSCC. The reason why is that the company is expected to provide an update on OS and DOR data from this trial after its oral ASCO 2025 presentation. The risk here is that despite the company showing that this FICERA combination beat historical benchmarks in targeting these specific HNSCC patients, there is no assurance that the OS and DOR data will turn out to be positive. Nor, that the stock market itself is going to end up viewing such data in a favorable manner.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the registration phase 2/3 FORTIFI-HN01 which is also using FICERA in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat 1st-line patients with R/M HNSCC. The risk here is that even if the OS and DOR data are ideal in the June 2025 ASCO release, there is no assurance that this will translate into a similar manner upon the release of final data from this particular FORTIFI-HN01 trial. Secondly, even though the company has gained alignment with the FDA that it could possibly file for Accelerated Approval of FICERA for this specific HNSCC patient population on the basis of an interim ORR endpoint, there is no assurance that the FDA will allow this at a later time. Plus, even if Accelerated Approval of this drug is granted, the company still has to achieve a positive OS data readout to keep this drug on the market.

The third and final risk to consider would be in terms of a possible expansion opportunity for FICERA. This would include the fact that the biotech intends to evaluate this drug in combination with KEYTRUDA to treat HPV-positive patients with a history of smoking. Such a trial is expected to start in 2025. The risk here is that even though the FICERA combination has worked well in the targeting of HPV-negative HNSCC patients, there is no assurance that a similar positive outcome will be observed when targeting these other groups of patients.

Conclusion

Bicara Therapeutics has done well to advance its bifunctional EGFR/TGF-beta antibody FICERA for the treatment of patients with 1st-line R/M HNSCC. This is evidenced by the updated interim data released as part of an ASCO 2025 Abstract presentation. As I have shown above, this particular drug combination was able to achieve 64% of negative HPV patients. I believe that before punishing this company, it would be prudent to at least see if the important OS number ends up being good after the release of the full data set.

This is going to be far more important than the interim data that was just released from this phase 1/1b study for this program. With the full data set from the phase 1/1b study using FICERA in combination with KEYTRUDA targeting 1st-line R/M HNSCC patients to be released at ASCO 2025, plus the gaining of alignment with the FDA that ORR is adequate for an Accelerated Approval pathway of FICERA for these patients, I believe that investors could benefit from any potential gains made here.

data.title: London Stock Exchange: No Room For Error
data.description:
Following our Euronext update, we are back to comment on the London Stock Exchange Group (OTCPK:LDNXF) (OTCPK:LNSTY). Euronext has been the best-performing exchange group in the EU, although the London Stock Exchange shares have also delivered double-digit returns (including the dividend payment - Fig. 1). As a reminder, we have been buyers of LSE with a strong recommendation called This Is A Game Changer; however, given its high valuation and Forex And Interest Headwinds, we downgraded the company to neutral in September 2024.

Fig. 1

Q1 Results and Our Take

The company reported a solid Q1 2025, with both top-line sales and gross profit increasing by approximately 8% year-over-year (Fig. 2). The company experienced growth across all business lines, with the strongest performance in the Markets and Risk Intelligence segments, which grew by 13.3% and 9.6%, respectively. Revenue aligned with consensus, with momentum driven by improving Tradeweb and D&A strength. The London Stock Exchange Group also slightly improved its gross profit margin. In numbers, COGS was reported as £308 million, which was 1% higher than consensus estimates. Gross profit consequently reached £2.04 billion. Going down to the P&L, profits were up 7.8% year-on-year, ahead of the consensus of +7.6%.

Source: LSE Q1 trading update - Fig. 2

Why are we still neutral?

Still a mixed view ahead. There are pros and cons to consider.

  1. Approximately 60% of revenues and 70% of EBITDA are generated in US dollars. Therefore, the recent US dollar weakness is a negative and will likely act as an earnings headwind for the future. Having said that, the company confirmed its 2025 guidance. The company's organic constant currency sales growth outlook is between 6.5% and 7.5% in 2025. However, Q1 sales were ahead;
  2. Results were broadly aligned with expectations, so we see a limited earnings upgrade ahead. As reported in Euronext publications and taking advantage of the April markets update, we see a substantial contribution from the Markets divisions due to higher volatility recorded and the US-to-Europe equity rotation;
  3. We still believe that the company is well-positioned to win market share in the structurally growing financial data market, thanks to Workspace/Refinitiv ahead of Bloomberg. This is supported by its product offerings and improvements with new features via the Microsoft partnership. Therefore, even in a challenging macroeconomic environment, the company's earnings might be isolated from potential headcount reductions, as it typically does not charge on a per-user basis. Still, LSEG's sales base is relatively concentrated, with a significant portion generated from a few major banks and financial institutions. Following the Q1 print-out, the company confirmed no fatigue in the sales cycle and confirmed its acceleration in top-line sales growth, while improving margins. For this reason, the company projects an EBITDA increase of 50/100 basis points in 2025;
  4. The company has a buyback ongoing, and £245 million of the £500 million stock repurchase program has been completed. Additionally, in March 2025, the company repurchased $250 million of its U.S. dollar-denominated bonds with a maturity date in 2031. This is a positive move to capitalize on a longer-than-anticipated high interest rate environment;
  5. Since Brexit and following the change of the non-dom tax regime, London has experienced a decline in its appeal as a leading financial hub. This trend could become more pronounced if European stock exchanges move toward further consolidation. The city has already seen a reduction in IPO activity and a decline in the number of companies listed on its exchange.
  6. We see a drop in the company's ROIC in the last few years. This is not promising, as it might be a precursor to the company's revenue growth slowing down. LSEG boasts a class-leading FCF Yield comparable to Euronext (Fig. 3), which is particularly impressive, given the capital-intensive nature of its Refinitiv-driven data and analytics business. It has the highest P/E ratio of all the other stock exchanges. For this reason, we remain on the sidelines.

Fig. 3

Earnings Change and Valuation

Following the results and the LSE 2025 guidance (Fig. 4), we see the company achieving a £9.45 billion sales figure. Market volatility and higher pricing in data analytics will support its sales generation and EBITDA margin development. Management reiterated an unchanged pipeline despite the recent market turbulence, reassuring Wall Street analysts and investors. On a negative note, we calculated that for every 1% decline in the US/GBP currency rate, the company's profit declines by at least 70 basis points. Looking at the past, FX had only a limited impact on the company's share price. We believe in the downtrend of the US FX, and from a sector perspective, we still prefer Euronext. Applying a higher-than-expected core operating profit margin of 36.6%, we derive net profits of £2.15 billion, with an EPS in pence of 406. Rolling forward our valuation and applying a 10% EPS CAGR over 2024-27, which is higher than the LSE historical average of +8%, we arrived at an EPS of 446 pence. Applying a lower outstanding share, given the ongoing buyback, our EPS grew by an additional 4%. In our last analysis, we valued LSE with a target P/E of 25x (Fig. 5), applying a 26x multiple on the 2026 EPS expected with a lower share count; our price is set at 11575p ($39.5 in ADR). The company trades at 11406p. Therefore, we continue to remain neutral. Our target price reflects an optimistic view (with higher-than-anticipated earnings growth), underpinned by constructive assumptions and the application of a higher valuation multiple.

Fig. 4

Fig. 5

Risks

Downside risks include 1) lower volatility with lower trading volume, 2) lower IPO activities and consequently a decrease in fee generation, 3) FX risks (as highlighted in the analysis), 4) wage inflation, 5) higher regulatory risks, including implementing new data regulation, 6) value disruption M&A, and 7) a meaningful valuation premium relative to peers.

Conclusion

The company is a leading global market infrastructure player and deserves a premium valuation. Having said that, even if we believe LSE is well-positioned to win market share in financial data and has demonstrated earnings diversification, the company is priced for perfection. There is no margin of error ahead with an EPS growth target above 10% and a current P/E target above 30x (however, this is not a company that generates >30% per year). Our neutral view is then confirmed.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: PCN: The Environment For Bonds Is Breaking Down
data.description:
The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund (NYSE:PCN) is a closed-end fund, or CEF, that provides a method through which income-oriented investors can achieve their goals. PIMCO, in general, is known as an asset management company that primarily focuses on bond funds. Bonds are typically considered to be income investments due to the simple fact that they deliver nearly all of their investment returns through the coupon payments that they make to their shareholders, as opposed to consistently increasing in price (as is the case with common stocks). We can see that this is the case by looking at the price and total return performance of the iShares iBoxx $ Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD), which tracks the Markit iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade Index, over the past ten years. Here is the chart:

As we can see, the price of the bonds that comprise the index has actually gone down by 10.21% over the past decade. However, it did still manage to deliver a positive total return over the period. This is because the coupon payments that the bonds made were sufficient to completely offset the price decline and still provide a return to investors. Therefore, all of the investment returns provided by corporate investment-grade bonds over the past decade were from the coupons that they paid out. Coupon payments provide income to their owners, so bonds were exclusively an income vehicle over the past ten years.

Therefore, as PIMCO is a bond house, we can expect that its funds will generally be targeted toward investors who are looking for income. Such funds can be expected to boast fairly high yields, and the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is no exception to this. The fund yields an impressive 10.69% at the current price, which is a phenomenal yield for an investment-grade bond fund. We can see here that it is far higher than the major domestic investment-grade bond indices:

Index/ETF

Current Yield

Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index (AGG)

3.85%

Markit iBoxx USD Liquid Investment Grade Index

4.52%

As we can clearly see, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has a yield that is more than double that of either of the major domestic investment-grade bond indices. In fact, it is higher than the 6.67% yield of the Bloomberg High Yield Very Liquid Index (JNK), which tracks domestic junk bonds. As such, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is clearly a more attractive income vehicle than any domestic bond index fund. The yield of this fund is also fairly attractive when compared to that of its peers:

Fixed Income – Taxable-Investment Grade

As we can see, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is the only fund in the peer group that has a yield higher than 10%. This is not surprising, considering that investment-grade bonds in general do not have particularly remarkable yields. After all, as we have already seen, the corporate investment-grade index is barely above a 5% yield and government bonds are even lower than that. In order to provide attractive yields, bond funds need to either use significant amounts of leverage to boost the effective yields that they earn from the assets in their portfolios or engage in fairly high levels of trading activity in order to get gains.

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund employs both methods to boost its yields, but it did not have an especially high turnover rate in its most recent filing period. For the six-month period that ended on December 31, 2024, the PIMCO Income Strategy Fund had a 24% turnover ratio. Its ratio for previous full-year periods was not much higher than that:

FY 2024

FY 2023

Portfolio Turnover Ratio

28%

29%

(Data is for the twelve-month period that ended on June 30 of the respective year.)

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund does not have a very high leverage ratio (0.75% as of March 31, 2025), so it is also not heavily relying on that to boost its yields. Given this, it is somewhat hard to see how it can sustainably maintain such a higher yield than its peers. We should investigate this and have a look at the fund’s finances for insight into exactly what it is doing.

Recent Performance

We most recently discussed the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund on March 10, 2025. There have been a few notable developments in the bond market since that time, and most bond sector movements have an impact on the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund.

One of the biggest, and most jarring, recent events was the downgrading of the United States Treasury by Moody’s. While everybody knew that this was coming, it still preceded a very bad twenty-year U.S. Treasury auction that also caused yields on both the ten-year U.S. Treasury note and the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bond to spike. Business Insider presents this chart of the yield of the U.S. Treasury since the start of April:

At the start of April (April 2), President Trump unveiled a series of sweeping tariffs that would prompt analysts at several major investment banks to predict that the United States would enter a recession before the end of the year. This prompted a flight to safety, which included investors buying U.S. Treasury securities. As we can see here, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note (US10Y) fell in early April from 4.35% on March 27 to as low as 3.94% on April 6:

The recession fears appear to have dissipated somewhat since that time though as the U.S. Administration has lifted the tariffs on many countries that were willing to renegotiate the trade deals that they have with the United States. This reversed the flight to safety and investors pulled money out of U.S. Treasuries and put it into riskier assets such as common stocks. This naturally caused yields to rise as less money was available to purchase bonds.

Yields have continued to rise since then due, at least in part, to the market increasingly questioning the “safe haven” status that has long been associated with U.S. government debt. The Moody’s downgrade means that there is no longer any rating agency that considers U.S. debt to be “risk-free.” While there have been some investors and analysts who have questioned the risk-free status of U.S. debt for a long time, the recent events have made it more real in the eyes of the market, especially international investors. As such, there has been upward pressure on U.S. Treasury yields, and that has pressured bond prices downward (due to the fact that bonds fall in price whenever yields rise).

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund invests primarily in corporate bonds as opposed to U.S. Treasury bonds. However, events that affect the U.S. Treasury market are still important for investors in this fund to monitor because they impact the performance of all bonds. In most cases, when the price of U.S. Treasuries falls, the price of corporate bonds will fall. As we can see above, the yield of the ten-year U.S. Treasury note is higher than the 4.19% that it had on March 10, 2025 (the date that my previous article on the fund was published). As such, we can expect the performance of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund to have been somewhat disappointing since our previous discussion on it.

This assumption is correct, as shares of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund are down 6.58% since the last time that we discussed the fund:

As clearly shown, this fund severely underperformed both the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate Bond Index and the domestic investment-grade corporate bond index over the period. This is not surprising given the fund’s use of leverage (leverage increases losses when asset prices decline). It will still likely be very disappointing to investors, as well as a turn-off to potential investors. After all, nobody likes to be holding securities that are declining in price and losing money.

The fund’s investors still took losses during the period, even when we consider the beneficial effects of its very high yield. As shown, on a total return basis, investors in the fund still lost 4.08% over the period:

The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund, as well as both bond indices, handed investors a loss over the period. However, the loss from the PIMCO fund was much worse than that of the index.

As I noted in the previous article on the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund, this fund was extremely expensive at the time as it traded at a price that was well above the value of the assets underlying each of the fund’s shares. It appears that at least some of the fund’s disappointing performance was caused by this, as the value of the fund’s assets did not decline to the same extent as the shares. As we can see, the fund’s net asset value only decreased by 2.68% since the date of our last discussion:

This illustrates the dangers inherent in purchasing any closed-end fund at a premium price. Whenever a sell-off eventually ensues, the fund’s shares do not have sufficient value underlying them to arrest the decline. In other words, funds that trade at a premium valuation can end up suffering very large declines whenever a sell-off occurs.

It is worth noting, though, that the underperformance of the fund’s shares relative to its net asset value could be a good thing for new investors. This is because the shares should be trading at a more attractive price today than they were back in March. We will discuss the fund’s valuation in a little bit, but this should mean that potential investors have a more attractive entry price today than they did previously. I will admit, though, that I am hesitant to recommend including bonds in a portfolio at all, given the threats to the U.S. dollar.

About The Fund

According to the fund’s website, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund seeks to provide its investors with a high level of current income with an emphasis on preserving its net asset value. That makes a great deal of sense for an investment-grade bond fund, given that investment-grade issuers rarely default on their debt obligations. As we can see here, even the worst investment-grade bonds only have a cumulative default rate of 4.64% or 10.29%, depending on whether we use Moody’s or S&P data:

The average default rate for investment-grade corporate bonds is less than 5%, which is quite respectable. Indeed, most investors will be relatively happy with that, and it is easily possible to effectively diversify that away so that an investor will not lose money over the long term. As we saw in the introduction, bonds deliver essentially all of their investment returns in the form of income to their owners (although it is possible for a bond fund to generate trading profits by exploiting changes in interest rates), so it makes sense that an investment-grade bond fund would advertise itself as providing both current income and capital preservation.

Interestingly, though, the fund’s website makes the following statement (emphasis mine):

The multi-sector fund seeks high current income.

A multi-sector bond fund is something of a different animal. As Morningstar explains:

Between traditional higher-quality core bonds and riskier high-yield corporate-bond funds lie strategies that offer investors a diversified approach to income generation with higher risk-adjusted return potential. Multisector bond funds bridge this gap by investing in a mix of fixed-income sectors (U.S. government, securitized, investment-grade corporates, non-U.S., and high-yield debt).

Morningstar has the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund classified as an investment-grade bond fund, which suggests that it holds primarily U.S. government and investment-grade corporate debt. That would be consistent with the fund’s secondary objective of capital preservation, although it would mean sacrificing a certain amount of income potential. The fund’s portfolio, however, looks like this:

We see that the fund has 38.07% of its assets by market value invested in junk bonds (High Yield Credit) and another 15.36% of its assets invested in emerging markets. That is 53.43% of its assets by market value invested in these two categories, and both categories are generally considered to be higher on the risk scale than investment-grade corporate bonds. This allocation suggests that the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is a multi-sector bond fund and not an investment-grade bond fund. However, it does appear to have a much higher allocation to investment-grade credit than other multi-sector bond funds. For example, the DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund (DSL) has this sector allocation listed on its website:

The DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund has 21.02% of its assets invested in leveraged loans, 7.29% in collateralized debt obligations, 35.90% in emerging markets, and 20.14% in junk bonds. While not all of these are necessarily below-investment-grade credit, the majority of outstanding debt in all four of those categories carries a junk rating. Thus, the DoubleLine Income Solutions Fund appears to have at least 75% to 80% of its assets invested in below-investment-grade credit and may have even more than that. This is much higher than what the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has.

Another way to look at a bond fund’s portfolio exposure is duration-weighted exposure. This basically tells us the extent to which a change in interest rates will affect the fund’s portfolio value. In the case of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund, 49.35% of its duration-weighted exposure comes from U.S. Government-related securities. We can see that another 8.00% of its duration-weighted exposure comes from investment-grade credit, and another 12.09% of its duration-weighted exposure comes from non-agency mortgages. This alone results in well over half of the fund’s duration-weighted exposure coming from investment-grade securities, which is very similar to what an investment-grade bond fund would possess.

We can therefore see that the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is not a pure investment-grade bond fund like some of the others that share the same Morningstar classification. However, it is still far more exposed to investment-grade credit and conditions impacting that market than a multi-sector bond fund would be. As such, we can continue to analyze this fund like an investment-grade bond fund. We should simply keep in mind that it holds several securities with higher yields and potentially higher default risks than most investment-grade bond funds.

Alternative Default Measurements And Concerns

The primary appeal that investment-grade credit has over junk-rated debt is the lower default rate. As we saw earlier, the average cumulative default rate on investment-grade bonds is less than 5%. However, non-investment-grade (junk) debt has an average cumulative default rate of 31.37% to 42.35%, depending on whether we use Moody’s or Standard & Poor’s data. However, this only refers to a particular kind of default – non-payment of principal and interest. There is a second form of default, and this second form of default is one of the biggest reasons why I am hesitant to recommend purchasing any fixed-income security, most especially investment-grade debt.

On Monday, Ray Dalio, founder of Bridgewater Associates, the largest hedge fund manager in the world, said that Moody’s and the other debt-rating agencies are missing a huge risk with U.S. Treasuries. According to Dalio, the credit rating agencies only take into account the risk of outright non-payment of debt, and he is correct. The risk of this kind of default is very low on investment-grade debt. However, bonds only require payment in nominal terms in order to avoid a default, not repayment in real terms. As such, a bond investor can lose purchasing power over the lifetime of a given bond even if the issuer does not default. This is a particularly big problem for investment-grade credit, as the interest rate paid by these securities might not be sufficient to offset the loss of purchasing power.

Former Wall Street analyst and financial author John Rubino has taken this theme even further. In an interview published Friday, he pointed out that the Federal Reserve appears to be losing control of interest rates. The central bank has been promising cuts this year, and the market appears to agree that this is the most likely scenario. However, as we saw earlier in this article, the ten-year U.S. Treasury yield has been rising over the past month or two. The thirty-year bond has actually seen its yield increase year-to-date:

At the start of this year, the thirty-year U.S. Treasury bond had a yield of 4.79%. Today, it yields 5.02%, an increase of 23 basis points year-to-date. This comes despite the fact that the Federal Reserve has been consistently stating that it intends to cut interest rates by 125 basis points by the end of 2027. The only explanation for this is that investors doubt that the United States will be able to repay its debts without resorting to money-printing and devaluing the U.S. dollar. This is the alternative default scenario, in that the debt will be repaid with a currency that is worth far less than was originally lent. Thus, the investor does not avoid losing money, as they could still very well lose purchasing power.

It is worth noting that all U.S. dollar-denominated fixed-income securities have the same problem in this scenario. As this scenario suggests a devaluation of the U.S. dollar, any bond that simply pays back the face amount at maturity will result in the investor getting back a lot less purchasing power than they originally lent.

Over the past year, we have also seen the Japanese government bond yield curve shift upward:

This tells us that the problem is not limited to the United States or to U.S. dollar-denominated bonds. However, it is certainly possible that there may still be ways for a fixed-income investor to preserve their purchasing power by purchasing bonds denominated in certain foreign currencies. However, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has very limited exposure to anything other than U.S. dollar-denominated bonds. Thus, this fund will likely be impacted should the scenario just described come to pass.

Valuation

Shares of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund are currently trading at an 8.60% premium to net asset value. This is better than the 9.61% premium that the fund’s shares have had on average over the past month, but today’s price still represents a premium on net asset value, and anyone who purchases the fund at today’s price is paying more than the underlying assets are actually worth.

The last time that we discussed the fund, it was trading at a whopping 13.71% premium to net asset value. As expected, then, some of the poor performance that this fund has delivered since early March was caused by the premium shrinking and not by the performance of the fund’s underlying assets.

Over the past three years, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has traded at pricing ranging from a 1.32% premium to a 29.00% premium to net asset value. The average price over the period was a 14.25% premium, so we can see that the fund is currently trading at a better price than that. However, it is still very expensive, which we can see by comparing it to its peers:

Fund Name

Premium/(Discount)

PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund

8.60%

BlackRock Core Bond Trust

3.91%

First Trust Mortgage Income Fund

(3.60%)

Invesco Bond Fund

(4.20%)

John Hancock Investors Trust

(8.33%)

Western Asset Investment Grade Income Fund

(3.29%)

As was the case back in March, the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is substantially more expensive than any of its peers. The price of this fund has admittedly come down, and a few of the fund’s peers are today more expensive than they were previously, but that has not been enough to close the price gap between this fund and similar closed-end bond funds in the market.

It is hard to justify paying a premium for any bond fund due to the likelihood of a stealth default due to money-printing. The PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has not historically traded at a discount, but it has had smaller premiums than currently.

Expenses

The fund’s most recent fact sheet states that the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund has a total expense ratio of 2.32%:

The majority of the fund’s expenses come from interest on the money that it has borrowed to obtain leverage, which is not particularly surprising. After all, most funds have leverage-carrying costs accounting for most of their expenses.

Conclusion

In conclusion, the overall environment for bonds and fixed-income securities appears to be deteriorating. There are signs that the Federal Reserve (and a few other central banks) have lost control over the long end of the yield curve, which is probably because investors are beginning to wonder how the governments of various developed countries will be able to afford to carry their current and projected debt loads without resorting to money-printing and currency devaluation.

This, unfortunately, makes it very difficult to recommend any bond fund right now. While the yield of the PIMCO Corporate & Income Strategy Fund is quite attractive, anyone who purchases it at the current price is overpaying for the assets that the fund possesses, and overpaying for bonds is very hard to justify.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IGLD: Safe-Haven Value Perspectives
data.description:
Introduction

FT Cboe Vest Gold Strategy Target Income ETF (BATS:IGLD) is a hybrid fund designed for investors seeking exposure to gold and steady cash flows. The fund articulates its performance objective through three clearly differentiated types of operation:

Treasury - 1-month Bills

The fund invests the majority of its assets in very short-term US Treasury securities (T-Bills). These instruments generate a ‘risk-free’ return that serves as a benchmark and provides stability to the vehicle in volatile market environments.

Exposure to the gold price via FLEX options on GLD

Rather than directly purchasing shares of the SPDR Gold Trust (NYSEARCA:GLD), the ETF does so through an affiliate in the Cayman Islands that purchases Flexible Exchange Options (‘FLEX Options’) referenced to GLD. On a monthly basis, the fund purchases longs of options (calls) to replicate 50-100% of the appreciation of GLD, and simultaneously sells options (puts or call spreads) to fund part of that exposure. At each option sale date, there is an ‘Upside Participation %’ that determines how much of the GLD rally the fund captures; it is typically between 60% and 80% depending on premiums and volatility.

Generating premiums to cover the target coupon

By selling these options on GLD, IGLD earns a volatility premium, which is distributed periodically as a coupon to all investors in the fund. The objective is that, when added to the T-bill yield, the total flow should exceed the 1-month bill yield by about 3.85 p.p., before subtracting costs and expenses.

Analysis - Macro Fundamental

Liquidity - M2

Next, we will analyze several catalysts that have increased the value of gold in recent times. Then, based on this analysis and looking at the future outlook, we will make a recommendation on whether to buy/sell or hold the vehicle in question.

First, we focus on global liquidity and its relationship with gold. As can be seen in the following graph, in this last period, from December 2024 until today, there has been an increase in liquidity around the globe, mainly due to the powerful injection of liquidity by the Chinese central bank (PBOC) into its economy. All this liquidity injected into the financial system has caused major financial assets to appreciate in their own right.

As a telling point, I believe that this injection of liquidity has only just begun, so it is a factor to be taken into account for the future appreciation of gold. If we also take into account that an increase in M2 (money supply) tends to depress the value of traditional currencies, this favors the rotation of capital flows to safe-haven assets such as gold or bitcoin, which, just by holding them, acts as a hedge against possible monetary inflation (in financial assets) caused by this increase in liquidity.

All of the above can be clearly seen in the years 2020–2021, where gold appreciated by 40% after the intensive QE programs applied by the FED to solve the problems derived from the COVID - 19 pandemic. Moreover, in the case of IGLD, one-month T-Bills, with rates at historically high levels, reinforce the target coupon, providing an income cushion even if gold corrects.

DXY

Secondly, we looked at the performance of the dollar (DXY). As FT Vest IGLD is denominated in USD and unhedged, an appreciation of the dollar directly penalizes both the price of gold and the value of T-Bills when converted into another currency (e.g. euros). It is clear from the chart below that the local peaks of the DXY coincide with the local valleys of gold. During the first part of 2025 the DXY has fallen, which has accompanied a strong rally in gold. Moreover, Washington has incentives to keep the dollar relatively low - given its high trade deficit - which would continue to support gold and, by extension, the vehicle in question.

Central Banks Historical Purchases

In the third point, we will study the different actions of the major market players, such as the central banks. In the following graph, we have a history since 2022 of the changes in the price of gold and the purchases made by the world's main central banks. These central banks accumulate gold as reserves, and we can see how the historical purchases have increased since the aforementioned date. This is a good indicator for all those who are thinking of exposing themselves to the asset through an investment vehicle such as the one analyzed in this article.

VIX & MOVE Index

In recent weeks, both the MOVE Index (fixed income volatility) and the VIX (equity volatility) have seen significant spikes, reflecting the growing uncertainty over the tariff war and nervousness in equity markets. Historically, when these indices reach highs - such as in March 2020 or late 2024 - gold consolidates as a natural safe haven, rising sharply in the face of fears of Fed hikes or stock market corrections.

In this scenario, IGLD offers a unique combination: on the one hand, it participates in the gold rally via FLEX options on GLD; on the other hand, it uses one-month T-Bills to take advantage of high yields on short rates; and, in addition, it sells volatility premiums to finance a target coupon of around 3.8%. Thus, buying IGLD now not only provides protection against extreme volatility, but also captures gold's upside potential while generating an attractive income stream in an environment of macro and geopolitical uncertainty.

Tariffs Agreements

The uncertainty mentioned in the previous section is largely caused by the current tariff situation in the US. While it is true that the Trump administration has reduced its tariffs on Chinese goods from up to 145% to 30% and in return, China will reduce its tariffs on US imports from 125% to 10%, which will remain in place for 90 days to allow for further negotiations to take place, there is still no official announcement of a major lasting agreement. This suggests that it is quite likely that there will again be a lot of volatility in the markets, which we have seen clearly favors gold as it acts as a safe haven asset.

Conclusion

In conclusion and to end this article, we believe that there is a good opportunity to buy the vehicle at the moment, although gold is at record highs, due to the current macroeconomic situation, the more than likely existence of future volatility (higher premiums) during 2025 and the existing geopolitical and commercial tensions. All this added to an increase in reserve accumulation by central banks and a sustained growth in liquidity/M2, we consider gold and the ETF a good investment opportunity at the moment.

data.title: CommScope Holding Stock Is Up On Increasing Q1 Revenues, But Risk Is High
data.description:
CommScope Holding Company (NASDAQ:COMM) reported improved Q1-2025 earnings earlier this month with increasing revenues and net income. The company’s stock price is up 325% over the last twelve months and 40% over the last thirty days. The company manufactures and sells hardware and software for network infrastructure. Its products and services are in high demand with data centers. The company is undervalued compared to its peers, but its high debt to revenue ratio is out of proportion and a high risk. I rate the company as a Hold for now.

Q1-2025 Results

CommScope Holding reported $1.112 billion in revenues, an increase of 23% YoY and a 4.87% decrease QoQ. The company reported gross profits of $468.6 million, an increase of 58.8% YoY and an increase of 4.69% QoQ. Profit margin for Q1 was 42.13% versus 32.75% YoY and 38.28% QoQ.

The company reported a net income of $784.0 million versus a net loss of $333.7 YoY and a net income of $6.7 million QoQ. Market consensus for Q2-2025 revenues is $1.27 billion, representing an increase YoY and QoQ. The company reports in July. CommScope Holding expects higher revenues for Q2.

All operating segments experienced YoY growth. The company’s Connectivity and Cable Solutions (CCS) segment brought in the most revenues, while its Network (NICS) segment saw the largest increase YoY. The company provided the following breakdown of revenues by segment.

Demand from hyperscaler and cloud data centers drove growth in the CCS segment. Enterprise fiber products for data centers rose 88% YoY and contributed $213 million to the total. The company’s SYSTIMAX 2.0 product is part of this mix. The demand comes from data centers updating their network infrastructure to meet the demand of AI computing. Q2 revenue in this segment is expected to increase.

Wi-Fi 7 products and the company’s RUCKUS products are driving growth in its NICS segment. RUCKUS consists of Wi-Fi network solutions including hardware and software. According to the company, NICS segment revenue may decline in Q2.

The company’s ANS segment was driven by sales of DOCSIS 4.0 products and legacy license sales. Revenues in this segment are expected to increase for Q2.

Cash on hand decreased due to the divestment of the company’s OWN (Outdoor Wireless Networks) and DAS (Distributed Antenna Systems) business segments. The company used the proceeds from the sales of these segments to pay down debt and to postpone debt maturities until 2027. Total debt is around $7.2 billion. The company paid it down by about $2 billion with the sales of the segments. The company plans to reduce debt further by 2026.

The company did not report free cash flow for the quarter. It expects to report break even cash flow for physical year 2025. It did report free cash flow for 2024 physical year.

CommScope Holding expects minor issues with tariffs, somewhere between $10 million and $15 million of additional costs for Q2. The company has a large global footprint and sources its raw materials locally. Products for 80% of its US sales were manufactured in the US or a compliant country.
